title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt:
You have been asked to write a review for an English-language community magazine. Write a review of a community project, charity event, or social initiative that you have been involved in or have heard about. Describe what it does, evaluate how effective it is, and say whether you would encourage others to get involved.

Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Riverside Community Garden — Growing More Than Vegetables

Nestled between two residential streets in the heart of our neighbourhood, the Riverside Community Garden is one of those rare initiatives that manages to be simultaneously practical, beautiful, and profoundly important to the people who use it.

Established three years ago on a neglected plot of land, the garden is managed entirely by volunteers and provides growing space to over sixty local families. Produce is shared among members, and surplus vegetables are donated weekly to a nearby food bank. The project also runs workshops on sustainable growing and cooking — all free of charge.

What makes Riverside truly special is not the vegetables but the community it has built around them. The garden has become a genuine meeting point for people of all ages and backgrounds who might otherwise never interact.

If I have one concern, it is that the project relies heavily on a small core group of volunteers and needs more regular helpers to be sustainable long-term.

I would wholeheartedly encourage anyone with a few spare hours each month to get involved. The rewards — social, physical, and personal — are considerable. Rating: 5/5

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt:
Your local community organisation has asked you to write a report on a survey about volunteering and community engagement among young people in your area. Include findings about current levels of participation, explore reasons why many young people do not volunteer, and make recommendations for how to increase youth engagement in community activities.

Write your report.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Youth Volunteering and Community Engagement in Northgate District

Introduction
This report summarises the findings of a survey completed by 175 young people aged 16-25 regarding their participation in volunteering and community activities.

Current Participation Levels
The survey revealed that only 28% of respondents had volunteered in the past year, a figure significantly lower than the national average for this age group. Among those who did volunteer, the most popular activities were environmental clean-ups, sports coaching, and food bank support.

Barriers to Engagement
When non-volunteers were asked why they did not participate, the most frequent responses were lack of time due to study and work commitments (61%), not knowing how to get involved (47%), and a perception that volunteering required a long-term commitment they could not guarantee (39%).

Recommendations
The organisation should develop a flexible volunteering scheme allowing young people to contribute in one-off sessions. A dedicated online platform listing local opportunities with clear time requirements would significantly lower the barrier to entry. Schools and colleges should also be engaged as partners, recognising volunteering within academic programmes where possible.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You have read a local newspaper article about declining community spirit in towns and cities. Write a letter to the newspaper sharing your views on what has caused this decline, describing what a strong community looks and feels like, and suggesting practical steps local residents and authorities could take to improve community life.

Write your letter to the newspaper.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

Your article on declining community spirit resonated with me deeply, as I suspect it did with many of your readers. The erosion of local connection is not an inevitable consequence of modern life — it is a problem with identifiable causes and practical solutions.

I would argue that two factors above all others have damaged community bonds: the replacement of local shops and gathering places with anonymous retail parks, and the rise of digital communication as a substitute for physical interaction. When people stop bumping into each other, they stop knowing each other.

A strong community is one where residents feel genuinely known and supported — where people look out for elderly neighbours, where local events bring diverse groups together, and where public spaces invite lingering rather than hurrying through.

Restoring this requires effort from both residents and local authorities. Councils should invest in community spaces, support local markets and events, and fund neighbourhood programmes. Residents, meanwhile, can begin simply: by introducing themselves to a neighbour, joining a local group, or volunteering an afternoon.

Community is built by individuals who choose to show up.

Yours faithfully,
A Local Resident
